questions.question: Which three object types does Azure Key Vault store?
options: Secrets, keys, and certificates
Secrets, blobs, and tables
Passwords, tokens, and certificates
Keys, queues, and connection strings
questions.explanation: Key Vault stores three object types — secrets (arbitrary strings), keys (cryptographic operations), and certificates (X.509 with lifecycle). Blobs/tables/queues belong to Storage, not Key Vault.

questions.question: Under the Azure RBAC model, which built-in role gives a typical app the least privilege needed to **read secret values**?
options: Key Vault Reader
Key Vault Secrets User
Key Vault Secrets Officer
Key Vault Contributor
questions.explanation: Key Vault Secrets User grants read access to secret values. Key Vault Reader sees metadata only, Secrets Officer can manage (write/delete) secrets, and Contributor is a management-plane role that under RBAC does not grant data-plane read.

questions.question: What is the main reason teams use `DefaultAzureCredential` from `Azure.Identity`?
options: It encrypts stored secrets at rest with a customer-managed key
The same code authenticates locally and in Azure with no code change
It removes the need for any managed identity when running in Azure
It caches secret values so the app never calls Key Vault twice
questions.explanation: DefaultAzureCredential walks an ordered chain and uses the first source that yields a token — managed identity in Azure, the developer's az login/Visual Studio identity locally — giving dev/prod parity with no code change. It still relies on a managed identity in Azure, and it authenticates rather than encrypting or caching secret values.

questions.question: With the .NET Key Vault configuration provider, the secret named `Database--ConnectionString` becomes which configuration key?
options: Database/ConnectionString
Database.ConnectionString
Database:ConnectionString
Database__ConnectionString
questions.explanation: Secret names allow only `[A-Za-z0-9-]`, so the provider maps a double dash `--` to the colon `:` used for nested config. `Database--ConnectionString` becomes `Database:ConnectionString`, which binds via the Options pattern.

questions.question: Soft-delete on Azure Key Vault is always on and cannot be disabled.
questions.explanation: Soft-delete is now always on and cannot be disabled. Deleting a vault or object moves it to a recoverable state for a retention period of 7–90 days (default 90).

questions.question: On a vault configured for the Azure RBAC model, a user holds the **Owner** role on the vault but no Key Vault data-plane role. What can they do?
options: Read and write secret values, because Owner implies every permission
Manage the vault (delete it, change its firewall) but not read secret values
Nothing at all — Owner alone does not permit management-plane operations
Read secret values but not change any of the vault configuration
questions.explanation: Management-plane RBAC (Owner/Contributor) lets you manage the vault but, under the RBAC model, does not grant data-plane access to read secret values — that requires a role like Key Vault Secrets User. (Under access policies, Contributor could self-grant a policy, which is why RBAC is preferred.)

questions.question: A team needs to grant Key Vault access **before** the App Service that will use it has been deployed, and the same identity must survive blue/green slot swaps. Which managed identity fits, and why?
options: System-assigned, because it is created automatically along with the app
User-assigned, because it is a standalone resource you can pre-provision and share
System-assigned, because it costs less than a user-assigned identity
User-assigned, because system-assigned identities cannot reach Key Vault
questions.explanation: A user-assigned identity is a standalone resource created independently, so you can grant it access before the app exists (breaking the chicken-and-egg) and reuse it across slots. System-assigned identities don't exist until the resource does; both flavors are free and both can access Key Vault.

questions.question: When an app with a managed identity reads a secret, what scope does the SDK request the access token for, against Key Vault?
options: `https://management.azure.com/.default`
`https://graph.microsoft.com/.default`
`https://vault.azure.net/.default`
`https://storage.azure.com/.default`
questions.explanation: The SDK requests a token scoped to `https://vault.azure.net/.default`; Key Vault then validates the token's audience/issuer and the principal's RBAC role before serving the secret. The management.azure.com scope is for ARM/management-plane calls, not data-plane secret reads.

questions.question: An App Service setting is `@Microsoft.KeyVault(SecretUri=https://kv.vault.azure.net/secrets/Db--ConnStr/abc123)`. You rotate the secret (new version), but the app keeps using the old value. What is the minimal fix?
options: Grant the identity the Key Vault Secrets Officer role on the vault
Drop the version segment so the reference resolves to latest, or update the version and restart the app
Enable purge protection on the vault that holds the secret
Switch the vault from RBAC over to vault access policies
questions.explanation: The reference pins version `abc123`; references resolve at startup and a pinned version never advances. Drop the version to reference latest (refreshes on the ~24h cadence) or update the setting to the new version and restart. Roles, purge protection, and the access model are unrelated to which version is served.

questions.question: To make Key Vault values override matching keys in `appsettings.json`, you should add the Key Vault configuration provider **after** the appsettings provider.
questions.explanation: Configuration precedence is last-wins: later providers override earlier ones. Adding Key Vault after appsettings makes vault values take precedence over file values.

questions.question: Using the .NET Key Vault configuration provider, you register it without setting `ReloadInterval`. After a secret is rotated, when does the running app see the new value?
options: Immediately, because the provider subscribes to Event Grid
Within ~24 hours, on the platform's periodic refresh
On the next request, because the provider re-reads per request
Not until the app restarts, because secrets are read once at startup
questions.explanation: Without a `ReloadInterval`, the provider reads secrets once at startup, so a rotated value is only picked up on restart. The ~24h periodic refresh applies to unpinned App Service Key Vault references, not the .NET provider; the provider does not poll per request or subscribe to Event Grid by itself.

questions.question: An App Service has both a system-assigned identity and two user-assigned identities. The code below runs in that App Service. What is the most likely problem?
options: It always falls back to the system-assigned identity, which may lack the role
Token acquisition is ambiguous because multiple managed identities are attached and none is specified
DefaultAzureCredential cannot be used inside App Service at all
The vault URI must end in `/secrets/` or the provider throws an error
questions.explanation: When a resource has multiple managed identities, you must disambiguate by setting `ManagedIdentityClientId` (or use an explicit `ManagedIdentityCredential`); otherwise token acquisition is ambiguous. DefaultAzureCredential works fine in App Service, and the URI need not end in `/secrets/`.

questions.question: The code below works when deployed to Azure but returns `403 Forbidden` when run on a developer's laptop. What is the most likely cause?
options: The vault firewall blocks the laptop, so no code or RBAC fix exists
Locally the chain resolves to the developer's az login/VS identity, which lacks a Key Vault data-plane role
DefaultAzureCredential is simply not supported outside of Azure
The secret name uses `--`, which is invalid on Windows machines
questions.explanation: In Azure the chain resolves to the managed identity (which has the role); locally it falls through to the developer's az login/Visual Studio identity, which lacks a Key Vault data-plane role. The fix is to grant that user Key Vault Secrets User on the dev vault — no code change. DefaultAzureCredential is designed for exactly this local scenario.

questions.question: Which statements about Azure RBAC vs vault access policies for the Key Vault data plane are correct? (Select all that apply.)
options: A single vault can use both RBAC and access policies simultaneously for the same operations
Azure RBAC can scope permissions down to an individual secret
Vault access policies are a per-vault list capped at roughly 1024 entries
Under access policies, a Contributor could grant itself a policy to read secrets
RBAC role assignments take effect instantly with zero propagation delay
questions.explanation: A vault uses one model or the other (chosen by `enableRbacAuthorization`), not both. RBAC scopes down to a single secret; access policies are a per-vault list (~1024 entries) and a Contributor could self-grant a policy. RBAC role propagation can take a few minutes, so option 5 is wrong.

questions.question: An IaC pipeline deletes and recreates a vault with the same name each run. After **purge protection** is enabled, redeploys fail with a name conflict. What is the correct fix?
options: Purge the soft-deleted vault first, then recreate it under the same name
Change the pipeline to `recover` the soft-deleted vault instead of delete-then-create
Disable soft-delete so the reserved name is freed immediately
Give the pipeline identity the Owner role so it can force-delete it
questions.explanation: Soft-delete reserves the name during retention; with purge protection on you cannot purge early, even as an admin, so delete-then-create is impossible. The pipeline must `recover` the soft-deleted vault and update idempotently. Purging is blocked, soft-delete cannot be disabled, and no role overrides purge protection.

questions.question: A secret has an `exp` (expiry) date that has already passed, but `enabled` is still true. What happens when an app requests that secret's value?
options: Key Vault refuses to return the value because it is past its expiry date
Key Vault returns the value; expiry is metadata your app and monitoring must honor
Key Vault automatically rotates to a new version and returns that instead
Key Vault returns the value only when purge protection is disabled
questions.explanation: Key Vault does not block secret retrieval based on expiry the way you might expect — `exp` is metadata, so the value is still returned and your app/monitoring must honor and alert on it. There is no automatic rotation on expiry, and purge protection is unrelated.

questions.question: Which of the following are valid reasons a production team might prefer an explicit `ManagedIdentityCredential` over `DefaultAzureCredential`? (Select all that apply.)
options: The chain's probing of multiple sources adds startup latency
A single explicit source gives faster, clearer failures that are easier to diagnose
ManagedIdentityCredential encrypts the token at rest, which DefaultAzureCredential cannot
It avoids ambiguity by binding to one well-defined credential source
DefaultAzureCredential cannot acquire a managed-identity token in Azure at all
questions.explanation: DefaultAzureCredential's ordered probing adds startup latency and makes failures harder to diagnose; an explicit ManagedIdentityCredential is a single, fast, unambiguous source. Neither credential encrypts tokens at rest, and DefaultAzureCredential does resolve to the managed identity in Azure — so options 3 and 5 are false.

questions.question: You want automated, event-driven secret rotation: when a secret nears expiry, regenerate the credential at the source and write a new version. Which mechanism triggers the rotation logic?
options: Key Vault polls the source system directly and rewrites the secret itself
Key Vault emits Event Grid events like `SecretNearExpiry` that trigger a Function/Logic App
The .NET config provider's `ReloadInterval` regenerates the source credential
Purge protection fires a webhook whenever the secret reaches expiry
questions.explanation: Key Vault emits near-expiry/rotation events (`SecretNearExpiry`, `SecretExpired`, `SecretNewVersionCreated`) via Event Grid, which trigger a Function or Logic App to regenerate at the source and write a new version. `ReloadInterval` only refreshes consumers; Key Vault does not poll sources itself, and purge protection has nothing to do with rotation events.
